spec_match.rationale: Targets the biggest XSLT casualty (pretty-printed feed pages) and shows DOMParser + template-string rendering as the modern replacement — matches CLAUDE.md's 'pair removal with a working example of the recommended replacement' guidance.

interactive.rationale: URL input, sample buttons, live fetch + parse + render, status panel. 5 controls.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Index promises 'fetches live RSS / Atom and renders it purely with DOMParser + template strings — the replacement for `<?xml-stylesheet?>`'. Page delivers.

edge_cases.rationale: Handles CORS failure with a helpful message; falls back gracefully on no items. Doesn't fall back when description contains HTML-escaped markup that the regex strip leaves messy; doesn't handle JSON Feed (despite the page being called 'jsonfeed-rss'); doesn't show what the old `<?xml-stylesheet?>` path used to render.

design_system.rationale: Inline style block uses CSS variables; channel title pulled via `style="font-family:var(--font-display)…"` is inline but variable-backed.

openQuestions.title: Add a real JSON Feed sample to match the slug
openQuestions.detail: The slug is `jsonfeed-rss` and the index mentions 'fetches live RSS / Atom' — but the page never renders JSON Feed. Add a JSON Feed (jsonfeed.org) example with a sibling `renderJsonFeed(json)` function so the slug isn't misleading and the page covers both modern replacement formats.

openQuestions.title: Show the pre-removal pretty-print for contrast
openQuestions.detail: Before XSLT removal, browsers applied a default stylesheet that turned `<rss>` into a readable column. A 'how it used to look' panel (just an image snapshot or a static markup mock-up) would teach what's actually being lost, not just the modern replacement.

openQuestions.title: Strip HTML from `description` correctly
openQuestions.detail:
`.replace(/<[^>]+>/g, ' ')` falls apart on CDATA, on `…

]]>`, and on entity-encoded angle brackets. Use a temporary `document.implementation.createHTMLDocument()` to parse the description fragment and extract textContent — safer and standards-aligned.

openQuestions.title: Add an enclosure / media element probe
openQuestions.detail: Podcasts ship as RSS with `<enclosure>` elements; that data is invisible in the current rendering. Surface a media-link row when present so the demo handles podcasts as well as text feeds.

summary: Tight DOMParser + template-string renderer for the canonical XSLT casualty. The page name promises JSON Feed but only handles RSS/Atom — adding a JSON Feed sample (and a 'how it used to look' panel) would round it out.
